death.cause: EH: "killed in the Indian Wars." No date or location given.

military_service.war: Indian Wars (French and Indian War era)

sources.notes: Confirmed: Rhoda d. of Abner and Mary, Oct. 4, 1749 is the Taft wife

open_questions.question: Is Josiah Rawson (b.~1730s, m. Hannah Bass, Grafton/Warwick MA) a sibling or cousin of this entry?
open_questions.notes: The Rawson genealogy book (entry #25) documents Josiah Rawson marrying Hannah Bass of Braintree, residing in Grafton then Warwick MA. His son Lemuel (#163-4 in Rawson book) settled in Richfield, Summit County OH. Lemuel's son Dr. Secretary Rawson (b.1796) was Richfield's first physician (1824+), practiced 40 years, founded the town's first library (1825), and was active in temperance work. If Josiah is a Hooker-Rawson descendant (gen 4-5), then Dr. Secretary Rawson is a gen 6-7 Hooker descendant who settled on the same Western Reserve land as John Newton (HD2925) and Eunice Newton Oviatt (HD2934) — all Connecticut Hooker descendants converging in Richfield OH. Action: Match Josiah Rawson to Rawson genealogy entry chain in the project.

burial.plot_notes: Pine Grove Cemetery, Douglas, Worcester County, MA. Section R.

bio.notes: Second child named "Hooker Rawson" in the Rawson family — this one in the John Rawson household; died age 2 in 1741. Compare HD0282 Hooker Rawson (son of Edward Rawson, b.1749, survived). The name was carried across multiple Rawson branches.

notable.notable_blurb: Mendon minister; Algonquian translator; Cotton Mather classmate

quotes.text: To my Beloved Wife Mrs Susanna Rawson who tho last in mention yet deserveth the first in my best and sincere affections
quotes.attribution: Rev. Grindal Rawson, Will dated Jan 24, 1714

narrative_blocks.header: Named for Elizabeth I's Archbishop of Canterbury
narrative_blocks.body: He was named "Grindal" after Edmund Grindal, Archbishop of Canterbury under Elizabeth I — his great-great-grandmother's brother, per the Harvard commencement of 1678. At that ceremony President Urian Oakes called him "sprung from a most illustrious stock" and prayed that Rawson might "in learning, holiness and excellence of character, resemble both Wilson and Grindal."

narrative_blocks.header: Mastered Algonquian; preached and translated for natives
narrative_blocks.body: He learned the Algonquian language and preached sermons in it to local Nipmuc parishioners — translating Thomas Shepard's Sincere Convert, John Cotton's Spiritual Milk, and a Confession of Faith. Cotton Mather called him 'a Master that had scarce an Equal' in the language.

narrative_blocks.header: Ordered to sail with the Canada invasion
narrative_blocks.body: In July 1692 the General Court ordered four ministers, Rawson among them, to accompany the forces in the expedition against Canada, “to carry on the worshipping of God.” Whether he sailed with Sir William Phipps is unknown.

cross_connections.display_label: carried the Mendon pastor’s name into the Talcott line

bio.title: Reverend
bio.photo_notes: Gravestone portrait, Mendon burying ground — primary for authenticity.

burial.inscription: Here lies Inter'd ye Remains of Mrs. Susanna Rawson Relict of ye late Rev'd Mr. Grindall Rawson, Pastor of a Church of Christ in Mendon & Daughter of ye Rev'd Mr. John Wilson 1st Minister of ye Gospel in Medfield who changed mortality for life Aug. 23, 1691. She was born Dec. 1, 1664, & died July 8, 1748, in ye 84th year of her age.

sources.full: Gary Boyd Roberts, Ancestors of American Presidents
sources.label: Roberts 2009

narrative_blocks.header: Wrote Cotton Mather a eulogy for her husband
narrative_blocks.body: After Rev. Grindal Rawson died on February 6, 1715, Susanna wrote a long letter to Cotton Mather — a detailed portrait of her husband's forty-year ministry at Mendon, his Indian mission work, his character, and his final hours. His last words were "Come Lord Jesus, come quickly!" The letter was published in the Rawson Family Memorial. Cotton Mather quoted it in the sermon he preached at Rawson's funeral.

narrative_blocks.header: Her gravestone names her father, not husband
narrative_blocks.body: Her gravestone in Mendon reads: "Here lies inter'd ye Remains of Mrs. Susanna Rawson, Relict of ye late Rev. Mr. Grindal Rawson... and daughter to ye Rev. Mr. John Wilson 1st Minister of ye Gospel in Medfield." She outlived her husband by 33 years, dying July 8, 1748, age 83.

narrative_blocks.header: Ancestor of President Taft through both Taft brothers
narrative_blocks.body: Her granddaughter Mary Dorr Taft married Moses Taft, and her great-granddaughter Rhoda Rawson Taft married Aaron Taft — Moses's and Aaron's fathers were brothers. President William Howard Taft descends through the Aaron Taft line.

cross_connections.display_label: was her father-in-law, the Bay Colony’s secretary

political_offices.title: Secretary
political_offices.notes: First Secretary of Massachusetts Bay Colony; home on Rawson Street (now Bromfield Lane), Boston

sources.full: Ellery Bicknell Crane, The Ancestry of Rawson (1887)

notable.notable_blurb: First Secretary of the Massachusetts Bay Colony, 1650-1686

cross_connections.display_label: married his son Grindal; a Hooker descendant

bio.photo_notes: Portrait by the Freake-Gibbs Limner, 1670. NEHGS, Boston. See artworks: ART042.
bio.bio_blurb: Secretary of the Massachusetts Bay Colony for thirty-six years

narrative_blocks.header: He proclaimed New England’s first Thanksgiving, 1676
narrative_blocks.body: From the steps of the Boston Council House in 1676, Edward Rawson read the proclamation now remembered as New England’s first formal day of thanksgiving.

narrative_blocks.header: Bay Colony secretary for thirty-six years
narrative_blocks.body: Rawson held the secretaryship of Massachusetts Bay from 1650 until the arrival of Sir Edmund Andros in 1686, even countersigning the warrant issued for the arrest of the regicides.

narrative_blocks.header: His daughter became a Whittier heroine
narrative_blocks.body: His daughter Rebecca Rawson’s tale of a bigamous marriage and a shipwreck became the subject of John Greenleaf Whittier’s 1849 Leaves from Margaret Smith’s Journal.

parents.notes: Family related to the Greene and Hawley families of New England

parents.father_research_notes: Son of Rev. John Wilson Sr., first minister First Church Boston

narrative_blocks.header: Founded Medfield church — served forty years
narrative_blocks.body: He became the first minister of Medfield, Massachusetts in 1651 and served the congregation for forty years until his death in 1691. He had previously served as a colleague of Rev. Richard Mather at Dorchester, and received his education at Harvard College in 1642.

He attended Harvard College in 1642, effectively the first graduating class of the institution. His father Rev. John Wilson Sr. was a close ally of Rev. Thomas Hooker, and John Jr. married Hooker's daughter Sarah — bringing together two of the founding families of Puritan New England.

His father, Rev. John Wilson Sr., was the first minister of the First Church of Boston from its founding in 1630 until his death in 1667 — the man who formally excommunicated Anne Hutchinson in 1638 and who supervised the execution of Mary Dyer in 1660.

narrative_blocks.header: Fell four stories — taken up for dead
narrative_blocks.body: Cotton Mather records that as a child, Wilson "fell upon his head from a loft four stories high into the street; from whence he was taken up for dead, and so battered and bruised and bloody with his fall, that it struck horror into the beholders." His father's prayers were credited with his recovery. He went on to serve Medfield forty years as minister, physician, and schoolmaster.

career.notes: Studied at Eaton's school (introductory to Harvard). Testified to ill-treatment of scholars by Eaton.
Colleague of Rev. Richard Mather at Dorchester. Known as "the gracious and godly Mr. Wilson."
Settled 1651. Ordained pastor Oct 12, 1652. Also served as physician and schoolmaster. Served 40 years until his death.

narrative_blocks.header: Her son married her sister Mary's daughter
narrative_blocks.body: Her son John Wilson married Sarah Newton — the daughter of Mary Hooker Newton, Sarah's own sister. Both of Thomas Hooker's daughters had children of the same generation who married each other, making their grandchildren the first documented double-descent in the Hooker tree.

cross_connections.display_label: Her son John Wilson married his first cousin Sarah Newton.
was her father-in-law and Boston’s first minister
